microHypotheses.statement: Replacing `processWorkflow.start` dispatches with `ctx.scheduler.runAfter` + internal wrapper mutations/actions preserves correctness (terminal states and no double-apply) because existing lease and status guards already enforce idempotent progression.
microHypotheses.rationale: Handlers already check status and claim leases before progressing work, and duplicates are tolerated by early-return guards.
keyAssumptions: Domain-level idempotency checks cover all external side-effect boundaries.
No hidden reliance on workflow execution metadata for correctness.
validationCriteria: Replay/idempotency tests pass after replacing workflow dispatch with scheduler dispatch.
No increase in duplicate apply/error events per 1,000 requests.
critiquePoints: Action scheduling and retries can produce duplicates; hidden non-idempotent side effects may surface.
questions: Which code paths still assume workflow runtime retries rather than explicit retries?

microHypotheses.statement: Scheduler-only architecture needs explicit per-tick throttling/chunking to remain within Convex scheduling and action concurrency limits under bursty load.
microHypotheses.rationale: Convex imposes hard caps on scheduled function fan-out and action concurrency; scheduler loop can otherwise enqueue too much in one tick.
keyAssumptions: Peak due-work can exceed safe no-throttle dispatch volume.
Current workflow maxParallelism implicitly provides protective backpressure.
validationCriteria: Synthetic burst test stays below 1,000 scheduled calls per tick and below action concurrency limits.
Scheduler tick latency remains bounded at P95 targets during peak batches/jobs.
critiquePoints: Real workload may be low enough that throttling complexity is unnecessary.
questions: What is observed peak count of due queued/running jobs+batches per scheduler tick in production?

microHypotheses.statement: Removing `@convex-dev/workflow` reduces operational complexity only if equivalent observability/retry policy controls are reintroduced explicitly in scheduler and handler layers.
microHypotheses.rationale: Workflow settings currently centralize retry/backoff/parallelism; removing them without replacement risks poorer debuggability and recovery behavior.
keyAssumptions: Team depends on predictable retry policy and workflow-level visibility today.
Existing trace events are not yet sufficient for all operational diagnostics.
validationCriteria: Post-migration runbooks can answer: queued work, in-flight work, retry history, and stuck work detection without workflow APIs.
Mean time to detect/recover from stuck orchestration does not regress.
critiquePoints: If current operational practice already ignores workflow status APIs, this may be low-risk.
questions: Do current dashboards/alerts consume workflow-specific status surfaces?

uncertainties: Unknown production burst profile vs per-function scheduling limits.
Unknown operational dependence on workflow status metadata.
Unknown performance delta between workflow workpool dispatch and raw scheduler fan-out in this workload.
